grp_lisraya_color_scheme_rules_06.fallback_exception: if PNG compositing infeasible, ship live version and accept badge-flush-left degrade; all other styling (fill, radius, text, shadow) must remain identical
grp_lisraya_color_scheme_rules_06.intent: Provide a robust cross-client fallback while preserving the treatment's other rules.
grp_lisraya_color_scheme_rules_06.preferred_remediation: pre-composite badge as transparent PNG with shadow/radii/icon baked in
grp_lisraya_color_scheme_rules_06.rule_text: The 50/50 overhang relies on position:absolute, which Apple Mail / iOS / most web clients honor but Outlook (desktop, Word engine) ignores — there the badge drops to the top-left and the box won't be inset. For pixel-perfect fidelity across all clients, the brand-safe option is to pre-composite the white badge (with its shadow, accent radii, and icon) as a transparent PNG placed via the same absolute positioning, or bake the entire badge-on-edge treatment into the box's background image while keeping the text live. When that's not feasible, ship the live version and accept the Outlook degrade (badge flush at the inner-left edge); every other rule (Light Blue fill, 40px 8px 40px 8px radius, blue 800 text, drop shadow) stays identical.

findings.description: The entire 'Graphic Elements — Waves' pattern (two colorways: Sunshine/Gold and Sky Blue/Deep Blue; long/short variants; 70% transparency layering; masthead gradient device) has no corresponding subtype, asset, or semantic tokens in the catalog, unlike Gradient Callout CTA and Icon Callout Box which each got a dedicated subtype.

Tint Rules lists four specified tints (headline D/M 40%, Light Blue box 70%, Golden Sand box 30%, wave top layer 70%), but only three are tokenized. The wave top-layer 70% transparency value has no dedicated primitive/semantic token.

Pantone codes for the Primary tier (2145 C, 137 C, 7548 C) are entirely absent from the catalog, despite a brand-mandated rule requiring '70% transparency of Pantone 7548 C / #ffc60a' be maintained in all logo instances.

The only primitive token carrying Sunshine (#faa31b) is scoped/named as a CTA-fallback-specific entity (background_gradient_cta_fallback) though its key (color.primary.sunshine) implies general primary-color status. Sunshine is used broadly (chart emphasis, wave colorway, campaign headline colorway, masthead gradient stop) — no generic Sunshine primary token exists, causing an ID/key mismatch and functional omission.

findings.description: Source states the patient brochure content block pattern is the 'reference template for visuals, messaging, and overall tone of ALL LISRAYA materials' — broader than a pure 'layout' class scope; the rule's class may need to also flag messaging/tone scope or a cross-reference relation rather than being purely layout-scoped.

payload.scope_note: Applies as reference template for visuals, messaging, and tone across ALL Lisraya materials, not layout alone.

findings.description: Typography rules (Agenda for headlines/headers, Nunito Sans for body copy in print, plus their font-source declarations) are present in the source but have no corresponding rule in the candidate set. These should be extracted as separate typography_application rules.

payload.description: Agenda must be used for all headlines/headers; Nunito Sans must be used for all body copy in print.

findings.description: The CTA shape/treatment instruction (SVG pill shape with gradient fill) is a distinct governance rule (CTA styling) with no candidate rule capturing it. Missing cluster entirely.

payload.description: CTAs must use the specified pill-shaped SVG treatment with defined gradient fill.

findings.description: The CTA gradient in u_0012 uses colors #FFDF55 and #FAA21B, neither of which matches the three approved brand colors in u_0001 (#00529B, #FAA31B, #FFC60A). #FAA21B is a near-duplicate/hex-mismatch of the approved #FAA31B (Amber Orange), suggesting either a transcription error in source or an undocumented exception to the hard color hierarchy constraint. This conflict is not captured by the current rule cluster.

The color hierarchy rule is marked hard_constraint/must but the source text is purely declarative ('The three approved brand colors in hierarchy order are...') with no explicit governance language (no mention of scope, exceptions, or enforcement). No facet capturing scope (e.g., applies to all banner/brand materials) or exception handling (e.g., CTA gradient discrepancy) is present.

payload.notes: No explicit exception governance found for CTA-specific gradient colors; verify against u_0012.

findings.description: Hardness/polarity of 'hard_constraint'/'must' may overstate the source, which only lists an ordered hierarchy without explicit mandatory/prohibitive language. Consider verifying whether this should be a softer 'guideline' unless corroborated elsewhere in the bible.

No candidate rules were extracted at all from this blob despite it containing at least 8 distinct, independently-testable accessibility mandates (type size, line height, type scale, contrast, form labels/tap targets, animation/motion, media alt text, and a reference standard citation). This is a total clustering failure, not just a granularity issue.

payload.note: Create one rule per list item under Accessibility section, each scoped to brand-mandated/general unless a channel override is present.

findings.description: u_0006 bundles two separable rules: (a) a general animation-design principle (quick transitions, avoid excessive motion, pause control, responsive design across devices) applicable brand-wide, and (b) a channel-specific '[GENERAL email]' rule about GIF first-frame messaging and a ~5s autoplay cap with static fallback. These have different scope (brand-wide vs email channel) and should not be merged into a single rule.

rule_b.topic: GIF frame-1 messaging and autoplay duration cap

findings.description: Forms rule requires labels to 'always' be visible and tap targets '≥48px' — these are hard, non-negotiable mandates (no placeholder-as-label, no disappearing labels) but no hardness/governance facet (e.g., mandatory/no-exceptions) is captured since no rule exists yet.

The WCAG reference standard citation (u_0008) functions as a governance/authority anchor for the entire accessibility section and should be linked to each accessibility rule as an external compliance standard rather than left unclustered or treated as a standalone unrelated item.

u_0004 contains two related but distinct obligations (test contrast / never rely on color alone; proof all text for legibility) with implied hard polarity ('never') that should be preserved explicitly as a prohibition rather than a soft guideline once clustered.

u_0001 contains at least three distinct normative statements bundled into one rule: (1) person-first language required for patient/DTC materials, (2) 'patients' is acceptable terminology in HCP materials (a differing, softer requirement), and (3) a process obligation to confirm audience in every brief before writing. Collapsing these into a single copy_editorial hard_constraint/must rule loses the audience-conditional polarity and the separate briefing-process obligation.

new_rules.text: Use person-first language ("someone/people with dermatomyositis") in patient materials.
"Patients" is acceptable terminology in HCP materials.
Confirm target audience in every brief before writing.

findings.description: Polarity/hardness mismatch: the rule is tagged uniformly hard_constraint/must, but the source explicitly permits ('acceptable') the alternate term 'patients' in HCP materials — a lower-hardness, permissive polarity that is not represented in the current single-rule encoding.

payload.note: Restrict hard_constraint/must to patient/DTC scope only; HCP usage of 'patients' should carry guideline/may polarity.

findings.description: Rule lacks an explicit audience/scope facet even though the source heading ('Audience Segmentation') and body text ('Language rules differ by audience') establish audience as the primary governing dimension. No governance metadata captures that this rule applies specifically to DTC/patient materials as scoped by the guide.

Missed relation: the patient brochure is declared the master reference for 'messaging, and tone across all LISRAYA materials,' which directly encompasses the audience-based language rule in u_0001. No relation/cross-reference is captured linking the assembly rule (master reference) to the copy_editorial language rule it authoritatively governs.

A single rule (email_code_conventions) is used to cover six distinct source units describing at least four separable concerns: (1) section stacking/sizing (600px table, no margins), (2) section metadata declarations (background, DM flag, footnotes, CTA), (3) footnote symbol assignment-at-assembly logic, (4) locked-component adjacency restriction, (5) dark-section adjacency restriction, and (6) code formatting conventions (HEX/px/inline CSS/Outlook bgcolor). These are not one rule; they should be split into distinct rules grouped by topic (layout/stacking, metadata declaration, footnote assignment, adjacency constraints, code conventions).

Units 0004 and 0005 express prohibitions ('must not include decorative elements...', 'must not be placed adjacent to other dark sections') — polarity should be must_not, but the candidate rule is tagged polarity=must uniformly, misrepresenting these constraints as positive obligations.

Unit 0006 (HEX colors, px sizing, inline CSS, Outlook bgcolor duplication) is a coding/markup standard, not an 'assembly' concern like the stacking/adjacency rules in the rest of the cluster. Tagging class=assembly for this unit misrepresents its scope — it applies to code authoring, not section assembly/ordering logic.

Unit 0002 requires section metadata to declare whether a section 'establishes DM' (dermatomyositis) — a compliance/regulatory-flavored declaration — but no governance facet (owner, review/approval step, or responsible team) is captured for this declaration requirement in the clustered rule.

The heading unit 0000 designates this as GENERAL Solstice production rules (cross-brand), but the candidate rule is scoped only under brand lisraya with no indication that scope may extend beyond the brand or is inherited from a shared production standard — scope provenance is lost in clustering.
